teams.name: Belgium
teams.style: Possession-based 4-3-3 with rapid wide transitions through Doku and Trossard; De Bruyne as the No.10, Courtois anchoring a veteran spine.
keyPlayers: Jérémy Doku (Manchester City, LW — most dribbles completed in UEFA qualifying)
teams.form: Unbeaten through UEFA qualifying, then crushed Tunisia 5-0 in their final warm-up. OPENED the World Cup with a frustrating 1-1 draw vs Egypt — Emam Ashour struck; Lukaku's introduction caused a Mohamed Hany OG; then drew 0-0 with Iran at SoFi — Nathan Ngoy was sent off for hauling down Taremi in the second half, reducing Belgium to ten men; De Bruyne was anonymous and Belgium created nothing in open play across 180 World Cup minutes.
teams.outlook: TWO POINTS from two matches and a crisis of creation — Belgium have not scored from open play in 180 World Cup minutes. Nathan Ngoy's red card against Iran means he is SUSPENDED for the June 26 finale vs New Zealand. De Bruyne's last World Cup has yet to show his fingerprints. Egypt lead on 4 pts; Belgium must WIN vs New Zealand and hope Iran cannot beat Egypt. The glass is cracking around Anderlecht's old wizard.

teams.name: Egypt
teams.style: Compact, disciplined low-to-mid block built on the meanest defence in African qualifying, springing counters through Salah and Marmoush.
keyPlayers: Mohamed Salah (RW, captain — scored 67' vs New Zealand; 9 qualifying goals)
teams.form: Unbeaten qualifying campaign without conceding. OPENED the World Cup by leading Belgium 1-0 — Emam Ashour struck; Lukaku's introduction caused a Mohamed Hany OG; then delivered EGYPT'S FIRST-EVER WORLD CUP WIN: 3-1 over New Zealand in Vancouver — Surman's 15th-minute header briefly led NZ; Zico equalised (58'), Salah scored (67') and substitute Trezeguet's corner-header sealed it (85').
teams.outlook: GROUP G LEADERS on 4 points after Egypt's first-ever World Cup win in eight attempts. Salah finally scored at 34 — the moment Vancouver gave the world. Only a catastrophic defeat to Iran in MD3 while Belgium wins vs New Zealand removes Egypt from the Round of 32; a draw seals the passage regardless. The crystal sees Egypt at the summit; the June 26 night is a coronation with caution.

teams.name: Iran
teams.style: Deep defensive block packed with veteran know-how, direct vertical service to Taremi; historically scores just 0.72 goals per World Cup match.
keyPlayers: Mehdi Taremi (Olympiacos, ST, captain — 59 goals in 103 caps; yet to score in this tournament)
teams.form: Cruised through AFC qualifying to a fourth straight finals; preparations overshadowed by a difficult geopolitical backdrop. OPENED the World Cup with a 2-2 draw vs New Zealand — Ramin Rezaeian and Mohebbi rescued a point; then held Belgium to a goalless 0-0 at SoFi — Ngoy's red card reduced Belgium to ten men but Iran still couldn't find a winner with the numerical advantage in the final stages.
teams.outlook: Two points and still mathematically alive — but must WIN vs Egypt on June 26 to have any hope of advancing. The 0-0 against Belgium showed defensive solidity; scoring against Egypt's back line is the harder challenge. Iran must attack against a side that is happy to counter-punch; Taremi must score for the first time in this tournament.

teams.name: New Zealand
teams.style: Organised, physical 5-4-1, direct long balls and set pieces funnelled toward Chris Wood; competitive without the ball, limited creation with it.
keyPlayers: Chris Wood (Nottingham Forest, ST, captain — 45 goals in 88 caps)
teams.form: Dominated OFC qualifying. AT THE WORLD CUP: Elijah Just's brace and Chris Wood's two assists earned a stunning 2-2 draw with Iran; then lost 1-3 to Egypt in Vancouver — Finn Surman's 15th-minute header briefly led, but Zico, Salah and Trezeguet answered to complete Egypt's historic first WC win. New Zealand's defending couldn't hold once Egypt found rhythm.
teams.outlook: ELIMINATED on 1 point after two matches — the 2-2 draw against Iran remains the tournament's great shock, but the Egypt defeat closed the door. The June 26 farewell vs Belgium (without Ngoy due to suspension) is a curtain call; Wood and Just have earned their standing ovation.

groups.groupReading: Belgium sealed Group G's summit in the final hour of the tournament's most eventful evening — De Bruyne's 66th-minute thunderbolt from outside the box and Trossard's pair at BC Place Vancouver overturning a narrative of three goalless open-play matches in forty extraordinary minutes. In Seattle a late VAR decision overturned what would have been Iran's 93rd-minute winner against Egypt, a moment that denied the Pharaohs first place as decisively as the Red Devils' five-goal eruption claimed it. Belgium's +4 goal difference leapfrogged Egypt's +2 in the same window; the glass had named Egypt, and the tournament delivered Lukaku's power and De Bruyne's long-delayed chapter instead. Final standings: Belgium (1st, 5pts, GD +4), Egypt (2nd, 5pts, GD +2), Iran (3rd, 3pts, GD 0), New Zealand (4th, 1pt, GD -6). The wizard of Napoli's World Cup has finally begun.

matches.reading: I see crimson waves crashing against a wall of granite that has not cracked in two years — yet Doku's lightning splits stone where Salah's counters only scorch it. The pharaoh strikes once on the break, but the old wizard of Napoli threads the dagger before the Seattle rain settles.
matches.keyFactor: Doku's dribbling vs an Egypt defence that conceded zero in qualifying

matches.reading: A nation playing with heavy hearts beneath hostile skies meets the tournament's humblest pilgrims, and grief becomes fuel. Taremi's single golden touch is all the prophecy requires, for Iran's matches are carved in 1-0 runes.
matches.keyFactor: Taremi's class against the lowest-ranked side in the tournament

matches.reading: The crystal is murkier than it was: Belgium's wide press could not open Egypt's wall and Iran's defensive fortress collapsed against New Zealand's direct running — both sides arrive at SoFi nursing wounds and needing points. Lukaku from the start tips the quality balance, and Doku in Inglewood's noon heat should find the space Iran's back line leaked against Just; but Iran, under pressure and playing with nothing to lose, will break forward where they were cautious with the All Whites. A second Belgian goal seals it, though Taremi may find a first touch of fortune.
matches.keyFactor: Lukaku starting from the first whistle vs an Iran side that must now attack after failing to hold New Zealand

matches.reading: Under Vancouver's closed roof two reinvented sides collide: New Zealand's Elijah Just and Chris Wood — who combined for both SoFi goals against Iran — demand Egypt's centre-backs engage in the duels the All Whites have perfected. Salah, goal-less in the tournament but smouldering, has the quality to find the seams in a New Zealand block that was never asked to defend in MD1 — Wood's side spent the entire Iran game on the attack. Egypt's superior squad depth and Salah's clinical threat give them the edge across ninety minutes, but Wood will test the pharaoh's defenders early and often. Egypt's first World Cup win comes in the final fifteen minutes — but this is no procession.
matches.keyFactor: New Zealand's Chris Wood and Elijah Just counter-attack vs Egypt's Salah-led class — this is the group's most open fixture

matches.reading: The arithmetic has redrawn the battlefield: Egypt need only a draw to advance as Group G winners — Hossam Hassan will organise his block and invite Iran to come forward for the first time in this tournament. Taremi, anonymous in 180 World Cup minutes, now owns the weight of his nation's survival — he must find goals against a back line that held Belgium for 47 minutes and New Zealand to one goal. Seattle's closed roof suits Egypt's counter-punch perfectly; one Salah strike on the break seals the night and the passage both.
matches.keyFactor: Egypt happy with a draw — Iran must attack for the first time in this tournament, opening the counter-attacking spaces Egypt exploited against New Zealand

matches.reading: Vancouver holds Belgium's last rite — and the complications have multiplied. Nathan Ngoy, sent off against Iran, is SUSPENDED, meaning De Bruyne's back line faces Wood's aerial threat with a defensive gap. Belgium's creation crisis is total: 180 World Cup minutes, zero open-play goals, the Napoli wizard invisible. Yet New Zealand are eliminated and the scoreline arithmetic matters enormously to Belgium's tiebreaker with Iran — if both reach 5 points, goal difference decides the passage. De Bruyne must not merely survive tonight; he must score. Wood will score once from a Payne corner delivery, because Wood always does; but Belgian class finally asserts itself when the knockout stakes strip away all other options.
matches.keyFactor: Belgium with Ngoy suspended — defensive gap vs Wood's aerial threat; De Bruyne must finally score in open play after 180 goalless World Cup minutes

teams.name: Spain
teams.style: Positional-play 4-3-3, suffocating possession and high press; Euro 2024 champions on a 31-match competitive unbeaten run.
keyPlayers: Lamine Yamal (Barcelona, RW — scored in the 10th minute vs Saudi Arabia; first WC goal, one of youngest ever)
Rodri (Manchester City, DM, captain, 2024 Ballon d'Or)
Mikel Oyarzabal (Real Sociedad, ST — brace vs Saudi Arabia, 21' and 24')
teams.form: Qualifying: 5 wins 1 draw, 21 scored, 2 conceded. OPENED the World Cup with a 0-0 draw vs Cape Verde (27 shots, Vozinha stopped all); then DEMOLISHED Saudi Arabia 4-0 — Yamal 10', Oyarzabal brace (21', 24'), Al-Tambakti OG (49'); both Yamal and Oyarzabal were rested at half-time. Yamal's goal made him one of the youngest WC scorers in history.
teams.outlook: GROUP H LEADERS on 3 points — qualified to the Round of 32 after the 4-0 answered every question the Cape Verde stalemate asked. Yamal is fit and prolific, Oyarzabal in blistering form. The June 26 finale vs Uruguay in Guadalajara's 1,500m altitude decides only the order — Spain need only a draw to confirm first place.

teams.name: Cape Verde
teams.style: Aggressive, fearless 4-4-2 mid-block with nothing-to-lose counters; diaspora-built squad with no intention of parking the bus.
keyPlayers: Kevin Pina (scorer of 30-yard free kick vs Uruguay, 21')
Hélio Varela (FW — scored his first international goal vs Uruguay, 61')
teams.form: Historic first qualification. WORLD CUP DEBUT: held European champions Spain 0-0 in Atlanta (Vozinha masterclass, 27 shots faced); then held Uruguay 2-2 in Miami — Kevin Pina's 30-yard free kick (21') opened the scoring; Hélio Varela headed in for his first international goal (61'); Uruguay's Araújo and Canobbio rescued a point with two goals in two minutes at the end of the first half. Two World Cup matches, two draws, unbeaten.
teams.outlook: SECOND IN GROUP H on 2 points — ahead of Uruguay on goal difference (+1 vs 0). Cape Verde are the tournament's best story: two remarkable results against Spain and Uruguay confirm they belong on this stage. The June 26 finale vs Saudi Arabia: a win guarantees second place; a draw likely advances them unless Uruguay stun Spain by a big margin. Vozinha's gloves and the Blue Shark spirit are the real deal.

teams.name: Saudi Arabia
teams.manager: Georgios Donis (appointed April 2026 after Hervé Renard was sacked)
teams.style: Mid-tournament identity crisis: Renard's brave pressing game replaced by pragmatic, counter-based caution; 25 of 26 players from the domestic league.
keyPlayers: Salem Al-Dawsari (Al-Hilal, LW, captain — scorer of the 2022 winner vs Argentina)
teams.form: Coaching upheaval under Donis (appointed April 2026). OPENED the World Cup drawing 1-1 with Uruguay (Al-Amri's opener); then CRUSHED 4-0 by Spain in Atlanta — Yamal, Oyarzabal (×2) and an own goal sealed the match before half-time. The tactical discipline that held Uruguay evaporated entirely against Spain's full-strength attack.
teams.outlook: BOTTOM OF GROUP H on 1 point — effectively eliminated after the 4-0 humiliation. Only a win vs Cape Verde on June 26 combined with Uruguay not beating Spain keeps any arithmetic alive, and the scenario is near-impossible. Al-Dawsari showed quality against Uruguay but was invisible against Spain. Donis's experiment ends in Philadelphia.

teams.name: Uruguay
teams.style: Bielsa's trademark man-to-man pressing and vertical chaos — a tournament-high 147 high turnovers in CONMEBOL qualifying; the post-Suárez/Cavani era.
keyPlayers: Federico Valverde (Real Madrid, CM — engine and leader)
Ronald Araújo (Barcelona, CB — scored vs Saudi Arabia and Cape Verde)
Darwin Núñez (Al-Hilal, ST — yet to score)
teams.form: Mixed friendlies, then a 5-1 loss to the USA. OPENED the World Cup with a 1-1 draw vs Saudi Arabia; then drew 2-2 with Cape Verde in Miami — Araújo (44') and Canobbio (45+6') rescued a point after Cape Verde twice led through Pina (21') and Varela (61'). Darwin Núñez was on the pitch but could not change the game; two draws from two matches.
teams.outlook: THIRD IN GROUP H on 2 points — behind Cape Verde on goal difference (0 vs +1). Two draws from two matches means Uruguay must WIN vs Spain in Guadalajara on June 26 AND hope Cape Verde fail to beat Saudi Arabia. Darwin Núñez's conversion rate is the tournament's greatest frustration; Valverde has worked heroically alone. Bielsa needs one night where the press finally finds its second gear at altitude.

groups.groupReading: Spain sealed Group H with serene, unbroken efficiency — a 1-0 win over Uruguay in Guadalajara's 1,500-metre altitude confirming De la Fuente's machine wins matches at precisely the gradient the scoreline demands, no more. Cape Verde's three draws across three opponents of ascending difficulty — Vozinha's 27-shot masterclass against Spain, Pina's 30-yard free kick against Uruguay, and a composed 0-0 against Saudi Arabia — confirmed Bubista's Blue Sharks as the tournament's most remarkable debutant story. Uruguay, who needed a win and Bielsa's highest-press night, found neither; Núñez remains goalless across 270 World Cup minutes and the summer ends without the collaborative chaos the system requires. Final standings: Spain (1st, 7pts, GD +5), Cape Verde (2nd, 3pts, GD 0), Uruguay (3rd, 2pts, GD -1), Saudi Arabia (4th, 2pts, GD -4). The oracle reads Cape Verde's next chapter with genuine anticipation — the island that held two South American giants and the European champions advances to the Round of 32.

matches.reading: Under Atlanta's sealed roof the orb shows a thousand passes weaving a net around the brave islanders — courage is no antidote to Rodri's geometry. Pedri conducts, the Blue Sharks chase shadows, and even with Yamal wrapped in cotton wool the champions stroll.
matches.keyFactor: Spain's possession machine vs debutant nerves; climate-controlled venue suits tiki-taka

matches.reading: Miami's humidity tests Bielsa's running men, but a team that changed its general seven weeks before war cannot hide its confusion. Valverde seizes the second half by the throat; Núñez, chaos incarnate, adds the second. No Argentina-2022 lightning strikes twice here.
matches.keyFactor: Saudi coaching turmoil vs Bielsa's high turnovers

matches.reading: Back in the Atlanta sealed roof, and now it is Spain under existential pressure — Rodri's geometry and Pedri's control must unlock what twenty-seven shots could not against Cape Verde's Vozinha. Saudi Arabia drew 1-1 with Uruguay on MD1 and will not simply lie down: Donis's side showed shape and a cutting edge through Al-Dawsari. Yamal, expected to start this time and freed from the hamstring shackles, changes Spain's rhythm in the final third; two strikes find their way past a defence thinner than Vozinha's that was, but the three-goal procession the glass once showed has been washed away by the Cape Verde rain.
matches.keyFactor: Yamal starting for the first time vs a Saudi side with real shape under Donis — Spain need to win but the margin will be earned, not gifted

matches.reading: Bielsa's wolves must hunt against the same defensive wall that absorbed Spain's twenty-seven shots without conceding — and Uruguay know it. Valverde's long-range threat and Núñez's physical menace will ultimately be too much for Vozinha and the Blue Sharks to absorb for ninety minutes, and one moment of Araújo's aerial authority from a set piece breaks the spell; yet Cape Verde, now unbroken at this level, will find one counter-punch through Monteiro or Livramento before Uruguay seals it late. Two strikes for La Celeste, one for the island warriors who leave Miami with their heads held high.
matches.keyFactor: Uruguay's Bielsa press vs the defensive discipline that held Spain to 27 shots — Núñez must convert the chances his MD1 performance squandered

matches.reading: Philadelphia's dome hosts Cape Verde's coronation or Saudi Arabia's last gasp. Cape Verde enter MD3 in second place on GD (+1), unbeaten across 180 World Cup minutes — two remarkable results against Spain and Uruguay have given the island its greatest sporting nights. A Cape Verde win secures second place with absolute certainty, regardless of what Uruguay do. Saudi Arabia, crushed 4-0 by La Roja and in last place, must WIN and need other results to go their way — the arithmetic is near-impossible, but pride has no arithmetic. Vozinha's gloves against Al-Dawsari's left foot remains the key duel; but Cape Verde's counter-attack is now a known quantity and the island belief is unshaken. Two Cape Verde goals and a historic passage confirmed.
matches.keyFactor: Cape Verde needing a win to lock in second place vs Saudi Arabia playing for pride after their 4-0 humiliation — the island's belief vs the desert's dignity

matches.reading: At 1,500 metres in Guadalajara, the night carries genuine consequence: Spain need only a draw to confirm first place; Uruguay must WIN to overtake Cape Verde for second. Bielsa's high-press system — destroyer of Italy in qualifying, yet producer of only two draws so far — will attack from the first whistle because there is no other option. Valverde has worked through two matches without proper support; this is the night he either finds it or Uruguay's summer ends at altitude. Spain, calmly organising a draw with Rodri's geometry and Pedri's touch, are built precisely for this scenario. The orb sees Núñez finally scoring — his one great moment of the tournament — but Spain's metronome does not concede the second. A 1-1 draw sends Cape Verde through as Group H runners-up; Bielsa's summer ends with the press unfinished.
matches.keyFactor: Uruguay must WIN — they attack from kick-off; Spain need only a draw and will use Rodri's geometry to absorb the Bielsa press at 1,500 metres altitude

teams.name: France
teams.style: Pragmatic, transition-lethal 4-2-3-1; a tournament-hardened machine that has reached the final in four of the last seven World Cups. Mbappé central, Dembélé wide, Tchouaméni shielding.
keyPlayers: Kylian Mbappé (Real Madrid, ST, captain — brace vs Senegal takes his WC tally to 14 goals, two behind the all-time record Messi tied the same evening)
Ousmane Dembélé (PSG, FW, 2025 Ballon d'Or winner)
William Saliba (Arsenal, CB — playing through a back issue)
teams.form: Qualified unbeaten; OPENED the World Cup with a 3-1 win over Senegal at MetLife — Mbappé brace (66', 90+6') and Barcola put France 3-0 ahead before Mbaye's stoppage-time reply. Mbappé is now France's all-time top scorer with 58 goals and holds 14 World Cup goals.
teams.outlook: GROUP I CO-LEADERS on 6 points — through to the Round of 32 with one game to spare. Mbappé scored twice more in Philadelphia against Iraq (14' and 54') to reach 16 career World Cup goals, equalling Miroslav Klose's previous all-time record (Messi has since surpassed it with 18); Dembélé scored his first World Cup goal for the third. The June 26 summit at Foxborough against Norway decides group order — a draw advances France as first seed on goal difference (+6 vs Norway's +4), and Deschamps' pragmatic farewell machine is built precisely for this scenario.

teams.name: Senegal
teams.style: Athletic, physically imposing 4-3-3 with a granite spine and pace in wide areas; Africa's top-ranked side, though chance-creation can run dry against deep blocks.
keyPlayers: Sadio Mané (Al-Nassr, FW, talisman — returns at 34)
teams.form: Won AFCON 2025 on the pitch (title stripped by forfeit ruling). OPENED the World Cup with a 1-3 loss to France at MetLife — held 0-0 until Mbappé struck in the 66th minute; Barcola added a second and Mbappé sealed it in the 90th before Mbaye's stoppage-time header became the youngest African scorer in WC history. The 2002 ghost stays buried.
teams.outlook: ELIMINATED on 0 points after a painful 2-3 loss to Norway at MetLife — Sarr's brace gave the night its late drama but Haaland's two goals and Pedersen's opener proved too much for a Senegal side whose own scoring was too late. Mané never found the net across two matches; the June 26 dead rubber vs Iraq in Toronto is the final page. The stolen AFCON crown and a World Cup without a point is a hard chapter for African football's proudest roster.

teams.name: Iraq
teams.style: Deep organised block, set-piece menace, channel balls to Aymen Hussein; resolute, limited, emotionally turbocharged at a first finals since 1986.
keyPlayers: Aymen Hussein (ST — 8 goals in AFC qualifying)
teams.form: Beat Bolivia in the March playoff to qualify, lost June warm-up to Venezuela. OPENED the World Cup with a 1-4 loss to Norway at Gillette Stadium — Aymen Hussein scored in the 39th minute (Iraq's first World Cup goal in 40 years!), but Haaland's brace (29', 43'), Óstigård's header and a Hussein own goal confirmed Norway's class. Winless through seven World Cup matches.
teams.outlook: ELIMINATED on 0 points — seven goals conceded in two matches, three more from Mbappé and Dembélé in Philadelphia to extend the pain. Hussein's goal against Norway in the 39th minute — the Lions' first World Cup strike in forty years — remains the one beam of national pride that cannot be taken back. The June 26 dead rubber vs Senegal in Toronto is the final curtain of a debut campaign that gave Iraq one unforgettable moment.

teams.name: Norway
teams.style: Ruthless transition 4-4-2 — sit compact, then release Haaland, Sørloth and Nusa at terrifying speed; averaged 4.6 goals per qualifier, the highest by any European nation ever.
keyPlayers: Erling Haaland (Manchester City, ST — 16 qualifying goals)
Martin Ødegaard (Arsenal, AM, captain — fresh off Arsenal's first title in 22 years)
teams.form: Perfect qualifying (8W 37-5, including a 7-1 aggregate demolition of Italy). OPENED the World Cup with a 4-1 demolition of Iraq at Gillette Stadium — Haaland scored twice (29', 43'), Óstigård headed a third and a Hussein own goal completed the rout. Haaland is now Norway's joint all-time scorer with Rekdal after just one World Cup match.
teams.outlook: GROUP I CO-LEADERS on 6 points — through to the Round of 32 after a breathless 3-2 win over Senegal at MetLife. Pedersen struck first, Haaland buried two more (now 4 World Cup goals in 2 matches, tied with Mbappé for the Golden Boot lead); Sarr's late brace kept nerves shredded to the whistle but Norway held. The June 26 summit vs France in Foxborough is the defining night — victory over the farewell machine earns Norway the first-place bracket path they haven't held since 2002.

groups.groupReading: France closed Group I with the decisive declaration the crystal had promised — but the instrument was Dembélé, not Mbappé alone. The 2025 Ballon d'Or winner scored a first-half hat-trick in Foxborough including one fed by Mbappé's diagonal vision, Norway's Thelo Aasgaard providing the sole Norwegian reply before Dembélé completed his third; a 4-1 masterpiece that gives France the tournament's most dominant group-stage record by a major contender: nine points, ten goals, zero defeats. Haaland ended with five World Cup goals in three matches — the Golden Boot race sits joint between him, Mbappé and Dembélé — but Norway's 1-4 final-day defeat confirmed what the French press has always suspected: their defensive shape leaks against elite attackers, even as their own attack remains the tournament's most feared weapon. In Toronto, Senegal delivered the farewell verse Mané's generation deserved — a 5-0 demolition of Iraq that closed the ledger with dignity and fire. Final standings: France (1st, 9pts, GD +8), Norway (2nd, 6pts, GD +1), Senegal (3rd, 3pts, GD +2), Iraq (4th, 0pts, GD -11). The death-group delivered its finest two sides to the knockout stage, and the bracket will feel their weight.

matches.venue: MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford (New York/New Jersey)
matches.reading: The spirits whisper of 2002, when Senegal toppled France in an opener — but this French side is no fading champion, and Senegal's well of goals has run dry. Mbappé, hunting the all-time record, strikes either side of Koulibaly's heroic resistance; the ghost stays buried.
matches.keyFactor: Senegal's blunt recent attack vs France's elite transitions

matches.reading: Forty years of waiting end for Iraq beneath a hail of Norwegian iron — sentiment is sweet, but Haaland does not deal in sentiment. The deep block holds for half an hour before Ødegaard's scalpel finds the seam; the giant feasts twice, as he did sixteen times on the road here.
matches.keyFactor: Haaland against the weakest defence in the group

matches.reading: The orb shows no mercy in Philadelphia: Mbappé already has 14 World Cup goals — one brace away from tying the record Messi claimed at Arrowhead the same evening, and every tournament fixture for France now risks becoming a record-breaking stage. Arnold's brave wall absorbed four Norwegian goals on matchday one; a France side hungry to hurt the goal-difference ledger will hit before the half-hour. Dembélé and Doué rotate through the corridors Iraq cannot close; by the 70th minute the bench empties and the old lions are already thinking of Norway.
matches.keyFactor: Mbappé's record-hunt and France's goal-difference appetite against a ten-man Iraq Iraq conceded four to Norway

matches.venue: MetLife Stadium, East Rutherford (New York/New Jersey)
matches.reading: The Meadowlands collision: Norway, who eviscerated Iraq 4-1 with Haaland's brace inside 43 minutes, against a Senegal side that already conceded three to France and whose scoring drought persists. Solbakken's front pair — Haaland and Sørloth — present different aerial dimensions the Teranga defenders faced nothing like in MD1; Koulibaly will need his finest ninety minutes to contain them. Yet Mané is still Mané, and the wounded-lion energy of a stolen crown gives Senegal one moment of dangerous beauty — the orb sees one Teranga strike, probably from Ndiaye's diagonal, before Norway's relentless press and a Sørloth corner-header settle it. The Nordic juggernaut advances; Africa's third-place lifeline stays alive.
matches.keyFactor: Norway's Haaland-Sørloth aerial dominance vs Senegal's Koulibaly trying to hold a back line that already conceded three in MD1

matches.reading: The summit duel the fates have circled since June 11 — now confirmed between two sides both on six points, both already through, and both carrying the weight of a Golden Boot race that belongs entirely to them. Norway's seven World Cup goals in two matches (Haaland with four, a new high-water mark for the tournament) against France's six (Mbappé's 15th and 16th in Philadelphia equalling Klose's old career record — Messi at 18 now owns it outright) have charged Foxborough with the electricity the oracle foresaw. Haaland will score because four in two matches is not a run of fortune, it is a declaration; Mbappé will answer because a man with 16 World Cup goals does not coast when one more sets a new European record. Deschamps — in his final tournament act — will not lose a game he does not need to lose; Solbakken will not surrender ground he has bled for across seven goals. A goal apiece and a summit shared; France's goal difference (+6 vs Norway's +4) likely hands Les Bleus the first-place bracket road if parity holds at the whistle.
matches.keyFactor: Both sides through on 6 pts — Haaland (4 WC goals in 2026) vs Mbappé (16 career WC goals, now Europe's all-time record) in the Golden Boot duel; France's superior GD decides 1st place if it stays level

matches.reading: BMO's humid evening hosts a farewell between two eliminated sides, both on zero points, both carrying moments they will carry for decades. Senegal, who lost 2-3 to Norway despite Sarr's valiant brace, arrive in Toronto with Mané still goalless across two World Cup matches and a squad whose pride has been badly dented; Iraq, whose Hussein goal against Norway in the 39th minute was the first Lions strike in forty years, come north with dignity intact even if the ledger is empty. For both sides the meaningful arithmetic is closed. Mané's hunger is personal and real — his last World Cup must end with a goal in front of whatever BMO crowd remains to witness it — and Senegal's superior squad depth should prevail. Two Teranga strikes and the orb's final verdict; the match exists beyond points now, in the territory of memory and legacy.
matches.keyFactor: Senegal's superior quality and Mané's desperation for a first World Cup goal vs Iraq's resilience — both eliminated, playing only for pride and memory
